Good evening. I=E2=80=99m Marion Maneker, and you=E2=80=99re
reading Wall Power, the private email I write four times a week with Julie =
Davich about the inner workings of the art market.=C2=A0

As I was preparing to distribute Wednesday=E2=80=99s Inner Circle email (up=
grade here ( https://puck.news/my-account/billing/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Powe=
r+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2=

=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )), which included an interview with Mattia De Luca a=
bout his Giorgio Morandi show last fall
in New York, some major news broke: Christie=E2=80=99s would soon have a ne=
w C.E.O., Bonnie Brennan, its longtime head of the Americas. My phone start=
ed to blow up with speculation about why the Pinaults were moving Guillaume=
 Cerutti into a new role running their family collection. On the chessboard=
 where Cerutti=E2=80=99s selection is just an opening move, I=E2=80=99ve go=
t some thoughts regarding strategy and tactics below.

=C2=A0

But first=E2=80=A6=C2=A0

* Frieze Los Angeles
is on: Finally, some good news about L.A., where the art world is gearing u=
p to do its part to support its own. Moments ago, I received this statement=
 from a Frieze spokesperson: =E2=80=9CAfter careful consideration and exten=
sive conversations with galleries, partners, and city-wide stakeholders, we=
 can confirm that the sixth edition of Frieze Los Angeles will go ahead fro=
m February 20=E2=80=9323, 2025. Our hearts are with everyone affected by th=
e devastating fires in Los Angeles. Since the fair=E2=80=99s founding
six years ago, Frieze has been proud to support and be part of this vibrant=
 community. The challenges the city is currently facing only strengthen our=
 commitment to work alongside the community to rebuild and recover together=
=2E=E2=80=9D
* Yves Saint Laurent=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cintimate=E2=80=9D drawings for sale=
 at Sotheby=E2=80=99s: Sotheby=E2=80=99s design department is holding sales=
 coinciding with Men=E2=80=99s Fashion Week in Paris next week. They=E2=80=
=99ve got the last of the Karl Lagerfeld
sales, and some of Saint Laurent and his partner Pierre Berg=C3=A9=E2=80=99=
s keepsakes, such as Polaroids taken by Andy Warhol and some objects acquir=
ed from the Kugel gallery which are being sold by a friend of the couple. B=
ut the attention-getting items are a series of Y.S.L.=E2=80=99s erotic draw=
ings, which the couple=E2=80=99s foundation has authenticated for the sale.

Despite splashy
coverage ( https://wwd.com/eye/lifestyle/yves-saint-laurent-erotic-sketches=
-sothebys-auction-1236798529/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%=
2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_m=
edium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a=
 ) in WWD, Sotheby=E2=80=99s is being exceptionally discreet in its managem=
ent of these drawings. Less than a week before the sale, none of the lots a=
re visible ( https://www.sothebys.com/en/buy/auction/2025/yves-saint-lauren=
t-pierre-berge-une-collection-intime-pf2554?locale=3Den&utm_campaign=3DWall=
+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBE=
RS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm=
_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )
on Sotheby=E2=80=99s website. (Sotheby=E2=80=99s says all of the lots will =
be online next week.) In fact, there will be a private room dedicated to th=
ese intimate works during the public viewing=E2=80=94a surprising touch in =
an art world where Tom of Finland is regularly featured.
* For the cheapskates among you=E2=80=A6: If you=E2=80=99ve not yet availed=
 yourself of the opportunity to upgrade ( https://puck.news/my-account/bill=
ing/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=
=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm=
_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) to the Inner Circle, =
I=E2=80=99ve saved a sliver of my interview with De Luca to convince you of=
 the error of your ways. The Magnani-Rocca Foundation=E2=80=99s collection =
of 50 Morandis, assembled by Italian musicologist and art critic Luigi Magn=
ani, opened at David Zwirner last night. Here are some more of De Luca=E2=
=80=99s thoughts on Morandi:

Marion Maneker: Morandi was very active as a painter for 40 to 50 years, bu=
t there are
only around 1,300 paintings.

Mattia De Luca: He was exacting. How many would that make, 20 to 30 paintin=
gs a year? If you think about it, it=E2=80=99s not that much. Did you ever =
see pictures of the table where he painted? He put these very thin sheets o=
f paper on the table. He would place the objects and then draw circles arou=
nd the circumference of the bottom of the object. That way, he knew if he w=
anted to go back to the same position the day after or in a
different light, he would know where to put them back.

So he didn=E2=80=99t have a studio, where he could set up a still life and =
leave it there. He had to reassemble it every day?

There=E2=80=99s a beautiful show in Paris now at the Giacometti Institute c=
alled Morandi Giacometti. It ties the artists together very well. They were=
 both obsessive. They both had very small studios. They had a bed in the st=
udio, both of them. Morandi had a single bed, the easel, and
table.=C2=A0

There=E2=80=99s something about these images stripped down that makes it po=
ssible for people to feel strong emotions.

What we know is that during both World Wars=E2=80=94because he was there fo=
r both wars=E2=80=94he had a huge depressive crisis. So he was even more lo=
cked within himself and his world during those years. I think that is quite=
 evident also in the works themselves. The wars were very, very tough for h=
im.=C2=A0

But I discovered something that I didn=E2=80=99t
know, which is quite funny to imagine about Morandi. Everyone has this idea=
 of him being locked in that room, and all he did was paint. Which was true=
=2E But, also, he would go to the broth=
el every morning before starting to pa=
int. Obviously, sexuality was very different at the time. He would go have =
his encounters and then work all day in his room. So he did have some sort =
of life outside his four walls. I didn=E2=80=99t know this before. I just d=
iscovered it.

And now, on to the Christie=E2=80=99s subplot=E2=80=A6

The Way of Christie's

***************************************************************************=
****************************************************************
The Way of Christie=E2=80=99s ( https://puck.news/what-guillaume-ceruttis-p=
romotion-means-for-the-pinaults/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2=
81%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&ut=
m_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd=
12a )
***************************************************************************=
***************************************************************************=
***************************************************************************=
****************************************************************

News of Guillaume Cerutti=E2=80=99s ascent to run the Pinault Collection se=
t
off a predictable game of telephone in the art market, filled with all sort=
s of informed (and uninformed) speculation about what the family might be p=
lotting. Herewith, a summary of the inside conversation, from the Qataris t=
o the Arnaults.=C2=A0

Marion Maneker ( https://puck.news/author/marion-maneker/?utm_campaign=3DWa=
ll+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&u=
tm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )
Marion Maneker ( https://puck.news/author/marion-maneker/?utm_campaign=3DWa=
ll+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&u=
tm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

The announcement of Guillaume Cerutti=E2=80=99s ascent
to a new role as chairman of the board at Christie=E2=80=99s and president =
of the Pinault Collection=E2=80=94which the initial press release described=
 as =E2=80=9Ca new organization for the artistic and cultural activities=E2=
=80=9D of Fran=C3=A7ois Pinault and his son Fran=C3=A7ois-Henri Pinault=E2=
=80=99s holding company, Groupe Art=C3=A9mis=E2=80=94prompted a number of p=
eople in the auction business to contemplate the meaning of the appointment=
=2E After all, Pinault p=C3=A8re, who turns 89 this summer, is
approaching the inevitable destination for all mankind. His most cherished =
projects need a steward.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In that sense, Cerutti is the ideal person to take charge of Pinault=E2=80=
=99s three museums (two in Venice, one in Paris); the extensive art collect=
ion of 10,000 objects; and assorted residencies and initiatives. Since the =
Pinaults hired him to run Christie=E2=80=99s, eight years ago, he=E2=80=99s=
 restructured some important
parts of the business. Before that, he served in a variety of French govern=
mental posts, including as chief of staff to the minister of culture.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

But a larger clue may lie in his previous appointment. Cerutti spent five y=
ears as the managing director of the Centre Pompidou, which includes an art=
 museum that has become a symbol of Paris=E2=80=99s forward thinking, sophi=
stication, and cultural prestige.
Designed by a young Richard Rogers and Renzo Piano, the architecture was a =
harbinger of the institution=E2=80=99s status as the second largest museum =
of modern and contemporary art in the world, behind MoMA.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In France, the politics of culture are, of course, hugely important. Just l=
ook at the way both the Pinaults and Arnaults leaped into action to rebuild=
 Notre Dame after
it burned, in 2019, or how Bernard Arnault ostensibly piloted the Paris Oly=
mpics last summer. =E2=80=9CThe Pinaults have big plans,=E2=80=9D one close=
 Christie=E2=80=99s observer texted me after the news broke. But for what?=
=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The Pinault Collection has sponsored, spawned, and sent out into the museum=
 world many highly regarded shows. Nevertheless, it has been edged out by A=
rnault=E2=80=99s Fondation Louis Vuitton,
which has scored more attention and social prestige. In this view, Cerutti =
could do for the Pinault Collection exactly what he did for Christie=E2=80=
=99s: raise its metabolism and push it relentlessly to the forefront.

=C2=A0

Others, connecting the news to rumors of M. Pinault=E2=80=99s poor health, =
made different conjectures. Without his father=E2=80=99s personal passion f=
or collecting, they posited, Fran=C3=A7ois-Henri would be inclined to recon=
figure the
art and museums for Art=C3=A9mis=E2=80=99s interests. Although a great deal=
 of money was spent on the art, it=E2=80=99s not as simple as shutting the =
museums and selling the assets (not that there=E2=80=99s any indication Fra=
n=C3=A7ois-Henri would want to). Under Cerutti=E2=80=99s trusted guidance, =
the family=E2=80=99s private art collection could be transformed into a pub=
lic institution=E2=80=94maybe one to rival the Pompidou in carrying Paris=
=E2=80=99s reputation as the capital of art (and luxury and fashion) well i=
nto the 21st century. There might even be
significant tax breaks in it.

Cerutti=E2=80=99s Choice
------------------

But why would Cerutti give up a job he relished to run a smallish
museum? Some pointed to the fact that, despite his surname, the 58-year-old=
 is a Frenchman living in London, and Cerutti may be eager to get back in t=
he mix on his home turf. There=E2=80=99s no reason a few years in Paris bui=
lding up the Pinault collection wouldn=E2=80=99t give him a shot, with the =
right political breaks, at becoming minister of culture himself. What forme=
r chief of staff doesn=E2=80=99t dream of having the job he knows he could =
do better than his boss?

=C2=A0

Some former Christie=E2=80=99s employees, however, turned their thoughts in=
 the opposite direction: With Pinault gone and Cerutti back in Paris, would=
n=E2=80=99t the family begin to put out quiet feelers to sell the auction h=
ouse? The art world continues to speculate=E2=80=94without much evidence=E2=
=80=94that Fran=C3=A7ois-Henri is eager to part with his father=E2=80=99s o=
ther passion project. Even if that were so, however, a dispassionate review=
 of the Art=C3=A9mis portfolio=E2=80=94Kering, CAA, Christie=E2=80=99s, a
large chunk of Puma, several vineyards, a chain of restaurants that make Br=
etagne crepes (Pinault is from Brittany), and the Stade Rennais football cl=
ub=E2=80=94might favor keeping Christie=E2=80=99s in this day and age, when=
 fashion and art ricochet off each other in unexpected ways.

=C2=A0

The oldest parlor game in the auction business, after all, is the rampant s=
peculation about who might buy Christie=E2=80=99s if the senior Pinault
were persuaded to sell. And while it might be easier to sell Christie=E2=80=
=99s after the transition at Art=C3=A9mis from father to son, it isn=E2=80=
=99t clear if such a transaction would ever be easy.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Christie=E2=80=99s is locked into a highly competitive duopoly that can cau=
se wild swings in revenue from year to year. Even if it weren=E2=80=99t so =
competitive with Sotheby=E2=80=99s, the supply of art and the business cycl=
es still
make the company anything but predictable. The lack of steady=E2=80=94even =
steadily declining=E2=80=94revenue and returns likely makes a deal less app=
ealing to private equity.

=C2=A0

Fifteen years ago, Sheikh Hamad al-Thani, the emir of Qatar, floated the id=
ea of making a bid ( https://www.ft.com/content/14cd7d5e-df8b-11df-bed9-001=
44feabdc0?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_con=
tent=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F17%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action=
&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) for the
company in the Financial Times. Pinault didn=E2=80=99t flinch, so the Qatar=
is instead hired Christie=E2=80=99s C.E.O. Ed Dolman to run their museum au=
thority and glorify the state. Would the al-Thanis revisit the idea? There =
was talk that the al-Thanis turned down the chance to buy Sotheby=E2=80=99s=
 from Patrick Drahi. (They weren=E2=80=99t willing to meet his price, which=
 included a slight profit.) Again, this talk may just be conjecture and sch=
adenfreude. Almost no one has the
access to actually know this. But the rumors convey the curiosity and conce=
rn surrounding both the auction house and Cerutti=E2=80=99s transition.

=C2=A0

Anyway, to play out the thought, just for fun: if the al-Thanis were to buy=
 Christie=E2=80=99s while ADQ holds a significant stake in Sotheby=E2=80=99=
s, an interesting new dynamic could unfold in the marketplace. What would t=
he Saudis do? Buy Phillips?=C2=A0

=C2=A0

That said, there=E2=80=99s always a chance that some centibillionaire tech =
bro or art- hoovering finance sky lord could swoop in and make Fran=C3=A7oi=
s-Henri very happy. The only other obvious buyer is the man who tried to bu=
y Sotheby=E2=80=99s in the wake of the price-fixing scandal, in 2000, then =
pivoted to backing Simon de Pury in acquiring a then much-smaller (can you =
imagine it) Phillips auction house. Having
gotten burned in the auction business, and effectively abandoning his stake=
 in Phillips, it would be another two decades before he took a run at Gagos=
ian, only to wind up with a likely disappointing financial relationship rat=
her than an ownership stake. I=E2=80=99m talking, of course, about Bernard =
Arnault.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Personally, I don=E2=80=99t doubt the Arnaults would want to buy Christie=
=E2=80=99s. But if they expressed interest, would the Pinaults
ever sell to them? Welcome to the art world=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=8A

Okay. I=E2=80=99m exhausted after playing fantasy French billionaire. I
need to go lie down.=C2=A0

And, as you can tell from tonight=E2=80=99s story, I=E2=80=99m always happy=
 to hear from you. Hit reply or use 917.825.1391 on Signal, WhatsApp, or pl=
ain old SMS text. Try it. I=E2=80=99ll get back to you toot sweet.=C2=A0

See you Sunday,

ed by Italian musicologist and art critic <strong>Luigi Magnani</strong>, o=
pened at David Zwirner last night. Here are some more of De Luca=E2=80=99s =
thoughts on Morandi:<br/><br/><strong><em>Marion Maneker</em></strong><stro=
ng>: Morandi was very active as a painter for 40 to 50 years, but there are
only around 1,300 paintings. </strong><br/><br/><em>Mattia De Luca</em>: He=
 was exacting. How many would that make, 20 to 30 paintings a year? If you =
think about it, it=E2=80=99s not that much. Did you ever see pictures of th=
e table where he painted? He put these very thin sheets of paper on the tab=
le. He would place the objects and then draw circles around the circumferen=
ce of the bottom of the object. That way, he knew if he wanted to go back t=
o the same position the day after or in a
different light, he would know where to put them back.<br/><br/><strong>So =
he didn=E2=80=99t have a studio, where he could set up a still life and lea=
ve it there. He had to reassemble it every day? </strong><br/><br/>There=E2=
=80=99s a beautiful show in Paris now at the Giacometti Institute called <e=
m>Morandi Giacometti</em>. It ties the artists together very well. They wer=
e both obsessive. They both had very small studios. They had a bed in the s=
tudio, both of them. Morandi had a single bed, the easel, and
table.=C2=A0<br/><br/><strong>There=E2=80=99s something about these images =
stripped down that makes it possible for people to feel strong emotions. </=
strong><br/><br/>What we know is that during both World Wars=E2=80=94becaus=
e he was there for both wars=E2=80=94he had a huge depressive crisis. So he=
 was even more locked within himself and his world during those years. I th=
ink that is quite evident also in the works themselves. The wars were very,=
 very tough for him.=C2=A0<br/><br/>But I discovered something that I didn=
=E2=80=99t
know, which is quite funny to imagine about Morandi. Everyone has this idea=
 of him being locked in that room, and all he did was paint. Which was true=
=2E But, also, he would go to the broth=
el every morning before starting to pa=
int. Obviously, sexuality was very different at the time. He would go have =
his encounters and then work all day in his room. So he did have some sort =
of life outside his four walls. I didn=E2=80=99t know this before. I just d=
iscovered it.</li></ul></div>

px;line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The announcement of <strong>Guillaume Ceru=
tti</strong>=E2=80=99s ascent
to a new role as chairman of the board at Christie=E2=80=99s and president =
of the Pinault Collection=E2=80=94which the initial press release described=
 as =E2=80=9Ca new organization for the artistic and cultural activities=E2=
=80=9D of <strong>Fran=C3=A7ois Pinault</strong> and his son <strong>Fran=
=C3=A7ois-Henri Pinault</strong>=E2=80=99s holding company, Groupe Art=C3=
=A9mis=E2=80=94prompted a number of people in the auction business to conte=
mplate the <em>meaning</em> of the appointment. After all, Pinault <em>p=C3=
=A8re</em>, who turns 89 this summer, is
approaching the inevitable destination for all mankind. His most cherished =
projects need a steward.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In that sense, Cerutti i=
s the ideal person to take charge of Pinault=E2=80=99s three museums (two i=
n Venice, one in Paris); the extensive art collection of 10,000 objects; an=
d assorted residencies and initiatives. Since the Pinaults hired him to run=
 Christie=E2=80=99s, eight years ago, he=E2=80=99s restructured some import=
ant
parts of the business. Before that, he served in a variety of French govern=
mental posts, including as chief of staff to the minister of culture.=C2=A0=
</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0=
;margin-bottom:0px;">But a larger clue may lie in his previous appointment.=
 Cerutti spent five years as the managing director of the Centre Pompidou, =
which includes an art museum that has become a symbol of Paris=E2=80=99s fo=
rward thinking, sophistication, and cultural prestige.
Designed by a young <strong>Richard Rogers</strong> and <strong>Renzo Piano=
</strong>, the architecture was a harbinger of the institution=E2=80=99s st=
atus as the second largest museum of modern and contemporary art in the wor=
ld, behind MoMA.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</=
p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In France, the politics of cultu=
re are, of course, hugely important. Just look at the way both the Pinaults=
 and Arnaults leaped into action to rebuild Notre Dame after
it burned, in 2019, or how <strong>Bernard Arnault</strong> ostensibly pilo=
ted the Paris Olympics last summer. =E2=80=9CThe Pinaults have big plans,=
=E2=80=9D one close Christie=E2=80=99s observer texted me after the news br=
oke. But for what?=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0=
</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The Pinault Collection has spo=
nsored, spawned, and sent out into the museum world many highly regarded sh=
ows. Nevertheless, it has been edged out by Arnault=E2=80=99s Fondation Lou=
is Vuitton,
which has scored more attention and social prestige. In this view, Cerutti =
could do for the Pinault Collection exactly what he did for Christie=E2=80=
=99s: raise its metabolism and push it relentlessly to the forefront.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Oth=
ers, connecting the news to rumors of M. Pinault=E2=80=99s poor health, mad=
e different conjectures. Without his father=E2=80=99s personal passion for =
collecting, they posited, Fran=C3=A7ois-Henri would be inclined to reconfig=
ure the
art and museums for Art=C3=A9mis=E2=80=99s interests. Although a great deal=
 of money was spent on the art, it=E2=80=99s not as simple as shutting the =
museums and selling the assets (not that there=E2=80=99s any indication Fra=
n=C3=A7ois-Henri would want to). Under Cerutti=E2=80=99s trusted guidance, =
the family=E2=80=99s private art collection could be transformed into a pub=
lic institution=E2=80=94maybe one to rival the Pompidou in carrying Paris=
=E2=80=99s reputation as the capital of art (and luxury and fashion) well i=
nto the 21st century. There might even be
significant tax breaks in it.</p></div>

line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">But why would Cerutti give up a job he relished=
 to run a smallish
museum? Some pointed to the fact that, despite his surname, the 58-year-old=
 is a Frenchman living in London, and Cerutti may be eager to get back in t=
he mix on his home turf. There=E2=80=99s no reason a few years in Paris bui=
lding up the Pinault collection wouldn=E2=80=99t give him a shot, with the =
right political breaks, at becoming minister of culture himself. What forme=
r chief of staff doesn=E2=80=99t dream of having the job he knows he could =
do better than his boss?</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Some former Christie=E2=80=99s emp=
loyees, however, turned their thoughts in the opposite direction: With Pina=
ult gone and Cerutti back in Paris, wouldn=E2=80=99t the family begin to pu=
t out quiet feelers to sell the auction house? The art world continues to s=
peculate=E2=80=94without much evidence=E2=80=94that Fran=C3=A7ois-Henri is =

tyle=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-=
bottom:0px;">Christie=E2=80=99s is locked into a highly competitive duopoly=
